data.title: Essent Group: Employment Threats Still A Concern
data.description:
Essent Group Ltd. (ESNT) is driven by issues in the employment market, which we mentioned in our previous coverage. The issue of employment is a central market theme right now because it relates to the proliferation of AI and also influences other factors important to Essent's business, like rates, concerning their fixed income reserve portfolio, and obviously claims, as well as demand for mortgages and housing in general. Reserving has risen because of the fragility in employment, and while it appears under control for the moment, hence the pause on rate cuts, we still have concerns in general around job growth and want to stay on the sidelines with this until we are more confident about the tenor in AI and agentic AI development, which has become too important of a trading factor in US markets - one that Essent has not seemed to be affected by yet as maybe it should.

Results

They are seeing solid growth in general. Housing starts are picking up, and so are mortgage originations, which will be the most direct KPI for a private mortgage insurer like Essent. These have started to steadily pick up since 2023, although considerably lower than the low-interest COVID-19 interest rate period. More in line with pre-COVID levels.

Consequently, premiums earned and policies written are growing. Note that net investment income is also growing. Reserving is also growing, related to macro, which we will comment on.

Looking at the portfolio in fixed income for a moment, we can make some comments about how macro might influence the performance of their available-for-sale assets. Fixed maturity and short-term investments are both classified as available for sale and reported at fair value as of the balance sheet date. In other words, it would seem available for sale is how they classify everything that they are holding in their reserve portfolio, taking a look at the balance sheet and seeing that all their investments and investments available for sale and reported at market value coincide, not much at book value, it would seem. We also say right away that they don't seem to have almost any equity exposure in their available-for-sale portfolio.

They have quite a lot of longer-term assets with more than 5 years in effective duration, meaning around 7+ years in average time to maturity. In terms of the yield curve, around 7 years and beyond is where changes in YTM, as per the new rate-cutting regime of the Fed, start to have a decreasingly profound effect. Already from 7 to 10 years, the decline in YTMs over the last 12 months goes from 60 to 44 bps, where between 6 months and 7 years, the decline in YTMs is around 60 to a peak of 80 bps at the 2-year mark. After 7 years, the impact of longer-term and structural considerations around rates starts to matter more, while before 7 years is the sweet spot of duration sensitivity and leverage to rate cuts, which we believe have a decent chance of continuing despite the apparent pause by the Fed.

The argument for more cuts coming from the Fed, despite some improvements in unemployment rates that motivated the current pause on cuts, is the renewed job cut surge in January. Moreover, the low-hire and low-fire environment presents an issue both for employment and even just through employment anxiety on consumer confidence. These are downside risks to YTM, which is good for the bond portfolio. On the other hand, the higher reserves for insurance claims by Essent over the last couple of years would be motivated by the increase in unemployment rates as well. While some bps in cuts from here on out with a couple of years of duration sensitivity, maybe a 1 pct cut to rates in the medium term, might lift investment income by $100 million (around 60% exposure to medium-term rates, 1 pct point YTM declines and around 3 years in duration so 60%*1%*3 years*$6 billion FI investments = approx $100 million), it could easily be offset by further increases in claims associated with risks to the growth mandate as well, with provisions doubling since 2024. Moreover, the 40% exposed to longer-term rates could be hit by higher, not lower, YTMs associated with a weakening status of the USD as a reserve currency, where the debasement trade has been dictating the long side of the yield curve. This could dampen the investment gains.

Bottom Line

It's an issue of direction. Threats to the growth mandate are going to matter for a business that depends on the credit of households to service mortgages. There are threats to that, especially when structural changes to employment from AI can affect employment and an independent debasement trade affecting markets, and the considerable exposure of ESNT to the long end of the yield curve. Even if unemployment stays at the levels at the opening of this year and doesn't rise, which we think is possible given the sustained low-hire and fire environment, there is still room for provisions to climb a little. It's not a no-brainer bet, and in this market, that means we want to stay away.

data.title: Is 1.8x Book Too Rich? The Case For Owning Main Street Capital Anyway
data.description:
Over the last few years, I’ve begun writing in earnest about business development companies, or BDCs.

While an 'overlooked' sector by most of Wall Street, BDCs tend to provide large, robust dividends, and as more and more baby boomers start retiring, income investing has grown in popularity and significance among the broader investing public.

So far, here on Seeking Alpha, I’ve written about a number of the top business development companies, including Ares Capital (ARCC), Blue Owl Capital (OBDC), Blackstone Secured Lending (BXSL), Hercules Capital (HTGC), Morgan Stanley Direct Lending (MSDL), and more.

One stock that has somehow escaped my coverage is Main Street Capital (MAIN), the seventh largest BDC on US markets and far and away the best-performing firm over the last five years:

Unlike many of the other BDCs that I have covered – that use external management and floating‑rate secured loans – Main Street is organized somewhat differently. MAIN is internally managed, meaning that the fees charged to investors are materially lower.

Not only that, but MAIN focuses on the LMM space, otherwise known as the 'lower middle market.' Companies in this tier are often overlooked by other funds, given the potentially higher risk profile, lower check size, and general lack of liquidity.

However, MAIN hasn’t been put off by this cohort and instead structures opportunistic deals (occasionally with equity included) where it thinks it can add value. The results speak for themselves.

Over the last five years, and over many other time horizons, MAIN is one of, if not the best, performing BDCs on the market, even if the firm’s headline dividend yield is somewhat lower than peers.

In turn, the firm has been rewarded by the market with an unbelievably high price‑to‑NAV ratio of more than 1.8x. For reference, most other BDCs trade in the 0.95x to 1.2x NAV range. Why is this? Is it worth paying such a high price for MAIN?

Today, I’ll dive deep into the company’s strategy and recent performance, highlight the valuation, and make the case that shares are still worth buying, even with such a premium to the underlying book value.

Sounds good? Let’s dive in.

Financials

Let’s start with the basics.

In case you’ve never heard of business development companies—or Main Street Capital—before, MAIN is an 'internally managed' BDC that originates, holds, and services first‑lien debt and equity in lower middle market companies, typically businesses with $10–$150 million in revenue and $3–$20 million in EBITDA.

In a typical setup, a business in this size range in need of capital will come to MAIN, and the firm will loan money to the borrower at an agreed‑upon rate. Over time, MAIN has built up a portfolio of loans, each of which produces net investment income on a regular basis.

In addition to the company’s loan portfolio, MAIN also has a growing fee‑bearing asset‑management business. Essentially, MAIN started as an internally managed BDC, but in recent years, the internal management team has begun managing outside funds and taking a high‑margin, capital‑light fee off the top.

This is not dissimilar to how Blackstone (BX) acts with its own funds.

Typically, BDCs are best thought of as 'pools of capital' that make investments in private credit, pay proceeds to shareholders, keep some profit on the side to insulate against losses, and essentially recycle capital when loans mature.

MAIN is different. Instead of working from a mostly 'stable' asset base, the firm structures deals with LMM borrowers to occasionally include equity provisions. That is, MAIN provides a company with capital but doesn’t just lend money; it may also take a stake in the company’s future success. The strategy, to put it simply, has worked.

In my mind, the best way to think about MAIN is by looking at the following chart that the company published in their Q3 2025 earnings report:

As you can see, MAIN has managed to continually grow NAV per share, which in turn has boosted distributable net investment income per share over time.

The company does not pay out all the DNII it receives, which, in addition to the equity stakes, allows the company to compound its NAV internally over time.

In the most recent quarter, MAIN pre‑announced Q4 distributable net investment income of $1.07 to $1.11 per share, which came in ahead of the $0.99 consensus estimate.

Given that MAIN currently pays out monthly dividends in the $0.26 range (implying $0.78 per quarter), there’s plenty of room for MAIN to continuously pay dividends while reinvesting proceeds to compound at a higher rate:

Speaking of compounding, in the Q4 pre‑announcement, MAIN management estimated a return on equity of over 17% for both the fourth quarter on an annualized basis and for the full year of 2025:

We also produced extremely strong fourth quarter investment activity in our unique lower middle market investment strategy, resulting in an annual record for gross investments of approximately $700 million in 2025. Our fourth quarter results continued our positive performance over the last few years and resulted in an estimated return on equity of over 17% for the quarter and for the full year.

This is an extremely strong ROE, which has been driven by the company’s combined record deployment—roughly $700 million in 2025—alongside a normalization of private loan activity and continued credit performance.

If the pre‑announcement is anything to go off of, it indicates that street models are too low for MAIN’s DNII and ROE, which could power additional earnings upside and analyst momentum going into 2026.

In its niche, MAIN is also performing quite well, given the level of deployments we saw last year.

Finally, it’s worth noting that MAIN runs a relatively conservative book, and if the credit markets wobble in 2026, the firm has significant dry powder and flexible funding sources through which it can produce new originations at favorable pricing.

This, combined with the firm’s new asset‑management franchise, where it manages external funds for a high‑margin, capital‑light fee, should set the firm up for success and outperformance over the next 12 months:

Taken together, MAIN offers well‑covered and rising dividends per share, NAV compounding, incredible mid‑teens ROE, a conservative balance sheet, and emerging fee‑income leverage—all of which add up to a compelling opportunity.

Valuation

Now the complicated bit.

As I’ve outlined, MAIN is in a terrific spot when it comes to its organic business, loan portfolio, financial performance, and future growth trajectory.

That said, MAIN also trades at a significant premium to the sector.

Recently I covered a number of top BDCs, including Ares Capital.

Prices have likely moved a little bit since my coverage, but at the time, ARES was trading at a 3% discount to book value, illustrating the underlying stress that BDCs have gone through in recent quarters as rates have come down and the market has soured on private credit exposure, especially to risky‑looking data‑center assets.

MAIN, by contrast, trades at a price‑to‑NAV ratio of roughly ~1.82x:

As of September 30, MAIN’s NAV per share was $32.78, and the stock currently trades halfway between $59 and $60.

Why such a premium? When you buy shares of MAIN, why does only 54% of your money actually go towards owning the loan portfolio?

The key here is threefold: the differentiated internal management structure, the NAV‑compounding focus, and the fee‑based management business.

As I mentioned earlier, most BDCs are relatively static pools of capital that make loans, pay out interest to investors, and then recycle capital when loans mature.

MAIN has a structurally different investment profile, with growing NAV, reduced payouts, and equity‑position upside.

This setup means lower current payouts to income investors but also provides a significant upward trajectory on NAV over time, which is rare and unique. This management strategy draws a higher premium, given that the capital is retained and compounded.

Not only that, but MAIN’s fee‑based business promises to drive significant new revenues that don’t require use of the firm’s equity base. As a result, the company trades at a significant premium.

In some ways, this may appear risky, given that MAIN is trading at the top end of its own historical range. Given the issues that other BDCs have had in terms of total returns, you might be forgiven for thinking that MAIN investors are exposed for when the 'other shoe' finally drops.

However, I would make the argument that MAIN will likely trade in this new multiple range - between 1.7x and 2x - going forward, as the company evolves from a simple pool of capital making loans to a more integrated business that uses its balance sheet and expertise to earn business across the spectrum.

Taken together, I see MAIN as a buy in the market right now, despite the frothy-looking price.

Risks

There are significant risks with owning a BDC, and MAIN is no different.

The core risk here has to do with the company’s loan portfolio, which is illiquid and at times may be marked inaccurately versus the true expected value of the underlying loans.

This is common with private loan portfolios, and there’s no real way to sidestep this other than to say that MAIN has historically run a conservative balance sheet and survived both the 2008 crash and the 2020 Covid meltdown without blowing up.

The second risk is genuine economic risk.

As macroeconomic forces take their toll on businesses, LMMs are exposed inordinately to pullbacks in consumer spending, slowdowns in business sentiment, and other factors. As such, MAIN’s NAV may fluctuate through economic cycles along, potentially, with dividend payouts.

Finally, MAIN may re‑rate back to a lower multiple in the event that the underlying credit situation worsens, loans begin underperforming, or the fee‑based management stream doesn’t perform as well as expected.

Summary

Overall, I see MAIN as a high‑quality, compounding private‑credit vehicle with significant upside through opportunistic deal structuring, a less competitive lending landscape, and the outside fee‑based business model.

Valuation is a concern, especially for those who may compare MAIN to other BDCs, but I believe at this price there’s minimal multiple‑compression risk as MAIN evolves beyond a simple capital pool towards a more integrated, growing, high‑ROE business.

Thus, my Buy rating.

Cheers!

data.title: GitLab: Punished Multiple, Compounding Business - A Setup For Asymmetric Upside (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
I wrote about GitLab (GTLB) before with a bullish tilt, supported by the company’s strong DevSecOps positioning and early traction AI features. Basically, GitLab was taking advantage of the consolidation in the enterprise toolchain consolidation trend while improving its operating leverage. The market has been merciless on the company since the “AI will kill SaaS and copilots will be commoditized” narrative gained traction. The company is attempting to graduate from a DevSecOps tool to a system of record plus orchestration layer company. GitLab is basically assuming that the game won’t be won by the best code completion tool, but rather by those who can provide end-to-end systems that can secure work and validation within. The task at hand is to graduate from a commoditized copilot to a production software platform that’s governance-friendly.

The question here is whether the market is punishing the business or if it is just punishing the multiple while the business keeps compounding.

What GitLab is

GitLab basically sells a DevSecOps platform, stretching from source control to security and compliance. The key here is the agents; they pull the heavy work. They can feed on context and create efficient production software. However, the problem here is the chain of custody. Why does the process exist? Has it passed controls? Does it create meaningful risks? These are all questions that need to be addressed in order to have software production at scale that also carries an audit trail. This is the difference between suggesting a couple of lines of code or having agents really taking action with enterprise-grade safety.

This basically tells us that coding is just a small part of the software production cycle. The real bottlenecks are in the planning, review, and control phases of the cycle. For GitLab to successfully go from an assist tool to an orchestration platform, it will need to be reliable at providing things that usually would need a lot of human labor. Things like fixing the pipeline automatically and enforcing policies in a way that allows it to effectively coordinate events across the lifecycle of the software.

Differentiation in Agentic AI

One thing is to sell “AI for developers,” and another is to sell to big organizations with rigorous procurement requirements and strict security needs. GitLab has a system of record that works as an advantage because it keeps track of the artifacts in the software production process. Their agentic workflows enforce permissions and compliance gates that can be scanned and properly audited, and that is critical for enterprises.

The big idea here is that the context is the real moat. The limiting factor is not code generation, but the right context. Does it know the prior incidents and security findings relevant to the current work, and can it act within permissioning? GitLab’s edge is exactly being able to be a source of truth that can constrain agents inside its permission model. If agents need to cross boundaries because of different security scanners or workflow systems, there is going to be a “tax” on latency and reliability. The company’s platform architecture addresses just that by keeping an inner loop. Execution happens where the data lives.

The business is executing

For all the talk about LLMs eating GitLab’s lunch, the financial picture seems to be representing a whole different company. Growth is strong at 24.6%, while the operating leverage seems to be improving, just like cash generation is inflecting. Basically, the financials point to a company where the cost structure is growing slower than revenue and the model is breathing health.

A couple of notes, however. The dip in the gross margin is far from problematic (high-margin business still), but it is a reminder that as AI workloads scale, infrastructure costs become a real dent (I have written on AI infra companies here). Obviously, the real risk here is that gross margin becomes a melting cube. Not our base case for now.

Net margin seems to have degraded YoY, but that is mainly optics on last year’s quarter benefitting from an unusual tax benefit. I would rather look at FCF, which, in our adjusted metric, is up.

Valuation

A look into the P/S ratio tells us that the company is being priced as a solid and cash-rich company, but not yet as a platform winner. The company is being valued below the category compounders (Datadog and JFrog) but above Atlassian and Elastic. Basically, the market considers GitLab a good asset, but it is discounting the orchestration upside until it is visible in the metrics.

To earn a multiple closer to Datadog and JFrog, the company will need to show evidence that the orchestration story translates into bottom-line gains.

Let me provide some color on what a price target might look like for the stock. Bearing in mind the analysts’ estimates for FY 2028, we get a midpoint of $1.33 billion for the top line. If we get a re-rate to a revenue multiple closer to ServiceNow (platform-like multiple for SaaS), then we would get a market capitalization of ~$9 billion (6.7x1.3B), which compares to the present $4.8 billion. Assuming a 2.7% annual dilution (same as last year), the share will be close to 173 million, and we get a rough price potential of $52.

What to expect in the upcoming earnings

GitLab will be reporting results soon; there are a couple of things that I would be watching. First, I would watch for revenue to keep YoY growth in the low-to-mid 20% range. That would be the story keeping its validity intact. But that is not enough.

Second, I would look at the cloud cost commentary, since in the last quarter, hosting costs were a cost driver. It is important to confirm that hosting costs are stabilizing, because if costs keep rising, the case for AI scaling means margin compression gains strength.

Finally, I would look for anything that may provide measurable metrics for the AI/agent strategy working.

Other than that, the market is expecting revenue of 947M, with EPS of $0.9, and I believe the market reaction is anchored on these figures, but it will mostly rest on the outlook sentiment coming from the call.

Risks

This story does not come without its own risks, and there are weak spots that investors should keep in mind. For starters, we are still in the early innings of AI development. Therefore, no one can clearly know how the landscape will evolve. It is possible that AI features become table stakes instead of monetizable differentiation.

There is a model dependency on third-party AI models. If we also consider that the AI industry is still in its early innings, as we mentioned before, then we cannot be sure how the infrastructure costs will evolve. That means high computing costs might undermine the current margin structure.

Another issue I urge investors to keep in mind is the obvious threat coming from competitors (Microsoft/GitHub) and the distribution and bundling power some of these might have.

Wrapping Up

If AI software production is going to be real, then companies will care less about who autocompletes best and more about who can safely and reliably ship software with tight governance. In this case, GitLab has a good chance with its system of record plus orchestration platform.

AI will not simplify operations; on the contrary, it will multiply them. Companies will deal with multiples of the current volume of code, repos, pipelines, and risks. That means a fragmented toolkit will be a tax on scale. GitLab’s platform is an ROI consolidation story, where the company’s platform reduces risk and reduces the “tool sprawl tax.”

So far we can see that the business is performing while the multiple is being compressed. The numbers reflect 25% top-line growth coupled with cash-flow inflection. This is not a sign of a broken SaaS model; on the contrary, it is a sign of a business scaling with success. If the orchestration story (lifecycle execution at scale with tight governance) ends up appearing in the financials, we will have conditions for asymmetric upside. If they can execute on this vision, the market can re-rate them as platform winners. If they can’t, you will still own a good-quality DevSecOps business with a working business model. Just don’t expect the market to pay you for optionality.

All said, I think the key aspect for investors is to understand how the sentiment could change to bullish, with the associated multiple expansion. I think that if the trend towards AI security and zero trust everywhere continues with free cash flow growth, the market will sooner or later recognize the company as a cash-generating platform with a moat.

data.title: The Bank of New York Mellon Keeps Its Bullish Case As FY 2025 Results Show More Upside Drivers
data.description:
It's time for another anniversary article, so today I'll be following up on The Bank of New York Mellon (BK), which I covered in Feb. 2025 and was bullish on. In fact, I called it a buy several times, and since my initial bullish call in May 2023, it is now up +199%, one of my best-performing ratings on this site.

Some common points across all my bullish ratings on this stock include capital strength, being a global systemically critical custodian bank, and also a compelling dividend story.

BK was also in another major financial site this week as JPMorgan (JPM) "raised its price target" on the stock and reaffirmed it as Overweight, showing what I think is continued confidence in this equity.

With roots dating back to the 1700s, BK is diversified across several business units, including "Securities Services, Market and Wealth Services, Investment and Wealth Management, and Other."

Today's follow-up will make use of my updated rating methodology for 2026 and consider data from the most recent earnings results in mid-January and try to give a holistic but concise review of this bank, which has been one of my favorites to write about in the last few years.

Thesis Summary

After my updated research this time, and considering new data and additional angles, my prior buy rating is reaffirmed yet again, and the below worksheet summarizes what categories drove this, with all sections overwhelmingly bullish:

This agrees with the latest Wall St. consensus, which is also a buy as of Wednesday, and my follow-up article today presents this stock as one that has impressive top-line growth drivers, improving operating margins, and continuing its status as a systemically critical bank with A-level credit ratings, as well as a compelling dividend story, and both my price targets and technical chart patterns support a bullish view.

Macro & Sector Outlook

To understand the macroeconomic/sector drivers of this stock, it's important to differentiate BK from banks that primarily serve the general consumer, as it does not. So, when thinking about BK, I'm thinking about factors that could impact institutional banking, trading/clearing, and money flows into managed assets and wealth advice.

Consider that in late January, a major financial firm like Morgan Stanley (MS) had a bullish view of the S&P 500, which I think could be a tailwind to equity assets under management if this forecast proves true.

As 2026 begins, Morgan Stanley’s Global Investment Committee expects the bull market to continue into a fourth year, projecting near double‑digit percentage returns for the S&P 500 Index.

Additionally, in the wealth advice space, the emergence of artificial intelligence/AI may not necessarily replace skilled and registered advisors but complement their role and drive some process efficiencies. Consider what McKinsey wrote in late January:

For wealth management, AI’s role may evolve from automation to agency, guided by advisor oversight.

Further, since Pershing has long been a major business of BK, providing "clearing and custody in 64 markets, execution in over 60 markets globally, and facilitating the trading and conversion of 46 currencies," I think that we should consider rising trading volumes in the near term could benefit this firm.

Trading volume may not just be driven by investor confidence that the S&P 500 will keep rising so they want to get in while possible, but also is driven by periods of extreme volatility, and "clearing" is necessary for both buys and sells.

I found interesting some points made just days ago in Seeking Alpha by Robert Berzins, CFA, who wrote that "2026 is about volatility," adding that some drivers of volatility may include a weakening labor market, continued elevation in the CPI inflation numbers, and also tracking the VIX and its patterns.

Given these points above and the role of BK as a major global institutional bank, clearer of trades, and involvement in asset management and wealth advice, I happen to be bullish in this section.

Competitive Positioning

Next, let's consider BK's competitive positioning among peers. I picked three comparable peers that are globally driven banks crossing the spectrum of custodian services, asset servicing, clearing, or asset management rather than consumer-driven or investment banking (M&A work, etc.): State Street (STT), Northern Trust (NTRS), and BlackRock (BLK).

In this market cap range of $27B - $175B, BlackRock leads with a market cap of nearly $175B, with BK trailing right behind it.

BK also trails BlackRock in five-year revenue CAGR at +5.3%, according to peer data, and it is also 2nd in this peer group with a return on equity/ROE of +12.9%, right behind Northern Trust.

Further, momentum data shows that BK beat a major benchmark like the S&P 500 in terms of 10-year price return at +327%; however, here again, BlackRock leads the pack:

Now, while many investors may see this data and think that BK is stuck trailing BlackRock, it's important to note in terms of competitive positioning that BK was included in the Financial Stability Board's 2025 list of global systemically important banks, something I've pointed out in prior years too, and this list does not include any of the peers above. So, it is important to view BK in the larger global banking context and not simply up against BlackRock, which Nasdaq called the world's largest asset manager since 2009, as BK is much more than just in the asset management space.

Given that BK beat the S&P 500 in the last decade, is a systemically critical bank, and has proven itself with a positive ROE and five-year revenue growth, I am willing to be bullish in this section.

Company Growth Factors

Moving on, we should narrow down to company-specific growth drivers and impacts.

When possible, I look for organic growth drivers and not simply rising markets. For example, in Q4 results (pg 2), the company attributed a +7% YoY revenue growth not just to higher market values but also to "net new business and higher client activity."

This is just the trend I am interested in. They had a similar result in full-year FY25 figures:

Fee revenue increased 6%, primarily reflecting net new business and higher client activity, higher market values and the favorable impact of a weaker U.S. dollar, partially offset by the mix of AUM flows.

Further, from the Q4 investor deck (pg. 21), I would note that BK has a positive top-line guidance for FY26, again driven both by organic and market growth, which I think should add to investor confidence:

On a more granular level, I think honorable mention should be given to the Pershing business, which I talked about earlier, since the investor deck (pg. 25) shows its YoY growth momentum in FY25, driven by net new assets, daily trading, and clearing:

In terms of Seeking Alpha data on expected fwd revenue growth, BK is around +6%, a positive sign, but still considerably below the sector average, so that could be an area of concern.

Given the multiple points above, I think the evidence supports a bullish case here too, particularly the organic growth, since it can drive future fees, but also Pershing momentum and the company's overall FY26 guidance. The bullish case will be supported if the bank continues to see organic client growth as well as market growth in the near term.

Profitability Metrics

Now, let's move beyond the top line and consider what could impact profitability. To compare the earnings efficiency of the core business, which in this niche is very fee-driven, I compared the operating margin as a metric of choice:

In this data, we see BK had an operating margin of +16.9%, putting it around the lower-middle of this peer group, and yet again BlackRock dominates with an operating margin above +34%.

Regarding net profit margin, which could be more impacted by interest rate impacts and other accounting items, BK did a little better here at +13.7% but was still beaten by BlackRock.

Now, it is only fair to BK to also note an impressive turnaround in its operating margin, if you consider the following chart, which shows how the margin has considerably improved since 2024 (which I highlighted with my green line):

The company had some positive remarks about its improvement trend in the Q4 investor deck (pg. 3), saying it saw "4 consecutive quarters of positive operating leverage in 2025 and eight consecutive quarters of positive operating leverage overall."

I expect if it can continue on this trend in 2026, it should continue to boost earnings.

Consider also from another angle beyond my own that the consensus has given this firm 12 upward earnings revisions vs. 0 downward and a consensus expecting 11-12% YoY EPS growth in FY26 and FY27.

So, I think that even though BlackRock has certain stronger metrics above, BK has shown an improvement trend, and there seems to be a lot of confidence in further profitability, justifying a bullish view in this section as well.

Balance Sheet Risk

In this section, we'll consider some balance sheet topics and their impact.

First of all, BK has a D/E of 1.20, on the higher end of this peer group, indicating greater reliance on leverage, and I think that moves the risk needle up somewhat:

Despite that elevated leverage, I would also mention that this stock is among my premium-tier watchlist, with A-level investment-grade ratings from all the major rating agencies, which tells me they continue to have strong confidence in the balance sheet:

In fact, Fitch said in its Jan. 2026 rating update on this bank that it "expects BK to maintain Tier 1 leverage above regulatory minimums and continue distributing excess capital in 2026," so I think that is certainly good news.

Further, like many other banks, BK also has a considerable amount of loans in its asset portfolio, as well as investment securities and trading assets. Of these, I think troubled loan exposure could have the greatest impact on investor and analyst confidence, particularly exposure to real estate lending. In that regard, notably from the Q4 remarks (pg. 2), they said:

Provision for credit losses was a benefit of $26 million, primarily driven by improvements in commercial real estate exposure and changes in the macroeconomic forecast.

To complement that, balance sheet data shows an improvement trend in the allowance for loan losses, comparing FY ending 2023 vs. FY25, which I believe shows greater confidence in the credit quality of the loan book now:

Given these points above, despite the somewhat elevated leverage vs. similar peers, I am a buy here as well, and the overwhelmingly strong A-level credit ratings send the needle over the edge for me.

Dividend Outlook

So far, every section I've reviewed has continued to support my ongoing bullish view of this bank. Now, we've come to the section on dividends to see if that changes the story.

At a 1.78% yield (as of Wednesday), it's not what I call a high-yielder and is actually well below its sector average.

Where it wins for me here is the 10-year dividend CAGR of +11.7%, which beats the sector average, and to visualize this, we see in the chart below that this bank was resilient enough to keep growing dividends despite market shocks like the 2020/2021 pandemic and, later on, the elevated inflation and interest rate period and the year of a few regional bank failures too.

So, though not appealing as a high-yield stock, it appeals as a resilience stock.

It also has a payout ratio of 26.6%, which leaves over 3/4ths of earnings available as a cushion, indicating greater dividend safety and lower risk of a cut.

Those points, along with an overwhelming consensus favoring further EPS growth, make this section a buy as well.

Valuation Metrics

In this section, consider my worksheet below, which uses Seeking Alpha peer data on valuation, as well as EPS estimates for BK, to compare valuation and also project a price target:

In this worksheet, we see both the fwd price/earnings multiple and the trailing price/book multiple are very slightly undervalued compared to the peer group average, perhaps as the market expects BK to perform close to peers. With a 2x price/book multiple, it also seems there is a premium on the quality of this bank's asset portfolio.

In fact, considering the strong EPS growth consensus and operating margin trend, this may actually be an undervaluation opportunity and deserves a higher premium, in my opinion, as a premium-grade, top-tier bank.

My price target implies a further +12% upside. Interestingly, the avg. Wall St.'s forecast implies +11.7%, so I am pretty close to theirs.

The numbers clearly justify being highly bullish in this section, which only reinforces the generally bullish tone of the rest of the article so far.

Technical Charting

As we approach the final stretch here, let's change angles and consider my technical chart:

In this chart, which compares the share price of BK over the last 1-year period vs. the 50-day and 200-day simple moving averages for the purpose of pattern recognition, it seems there are no clear buy or sell signals such as golden crosses (50-day move above the 200-day) or death crosses (50-day drops below the 200-day).

Instead, the moving averages have been parallel for a long time.

However, they are both continuing on an upward trend, and the share price seems to continually be above the 50-day SMA, which has been acting as a "support" line. In fact, the recent price dip shown did not fall too far below that support before coming back.

For this reason, I am more inclined to say buy than hold. As for a sell, there is little to no technical evidence supporting bearishness at this time.

While it does not happen in every article, but often does, this technical pattern definitely reinforces my overwhelmingly bullish article and keeps the overall rating in the buy category rather than hurting it.

Risks and Challenges to Thesis

Of course, we should also consider a risk that could challenge my bullish view. Since I've not talked about interest rate risk so far in this article, that is actually a huge potential challenge, not just to BK but to banking as a sector.

All eyes are on Fed decisions this year, which are, of course, outside the control of this bank, yet those policy rates can impact short-term funding costs. CME FedWatch predictions indicate a strong likelihood a further rate cut won't happen in March.

I would present to you that if short-term rates stay elevated (a “higher for longer” scenario), BK and other banks could face net interest margin headwinds if funding costs on deposits, repos, and other short-term liabilities remain elevated but longer-term interest income does not rise as quickly.

A mitigating factor in this case is that BK also has a robust fees-driven business as well that is not so exposed to interest rate risk, and that diversification beyond just corporate lending could help lower this risk factor, something some regional banks dependent solely on interest income and lending may not have the privilege of.

Conclusion

In conclusion, not only does my buy rating today agree with the Wall St. consensus, but it also continues to reaffirm my continuing bullish view of BK over several articles, despite different time periods and new data that comes out.

My call to action for readers is to keep this bank on your watchlist for further research on systemically critical global banks that are also a compelling dividend idea this month.

data.title: Tariff Decision Day?
data.description:
The US dollar (DXY) is trading mostly firmer but quietly in what could be a volatile North American session. It is not just about the US data, of which there is plenty - including the PCE deflator and the first look at Q4 GDP, the preliminary February PMI and the University of Michigan’s final February reading on consumer confidence. The Supreme Court will hand down some decisions today, and it's possible that it rules on the president’s use of emergency powers to impose widespread tariffs. The event markets suggest a strong majority expect the high court to rule against most all of the tariffs that have been justified by the International Economic Emergency Powers Act (IEEPA). Tensions between the US and Iran remain elevated, but the threat of an imminent strike has relaxed a little and oil prices are a little softer ahead of the weekend.

The dollar has risen against the G10 currencies this week. The yen has been the weakest, and it is off almost 1.65%. The Australian dollar has been the best performer, and it is off about 0.3% this week. Most emerging market currencies also declined this week. The Argentine peso (~0.6%), Russian ruble (~0.2%), and Brazilian real (0.15%) have been the best performers.

Prices

G10

• The euro was sold to almost $1.1740 in North America yesterday, a four-week low. It has held barely above it today. After the low was recorded, the euro was unable to resurface above $1.1780, around Wednesday’s low, and this is also holding. Options for one billion euros at $1.1775 expire today. The next area of chart support is in the $1.1670-1.1700 area. The euro settled slightly below $1.1870 at the end of last week.

• The dollar reached a seven-session high against the Japanese yen today near JPY155.65 in late Asia/early European turnover. It has come off to around JPY155.15 in the European morning. The session low was near JPY154.90. Still, the dollar’s technical tone is constructive, and overcoming the JPY155.65-80 area could target the JPY156.50-65 area next.

• Sterling was sold to a four-week low yesterday near $1.3435. While fraying the 200-day moving average (~$1.3445), sterling met the (50%) retracement of its 8.5-cent rally from early last November’s low (~$1.3010) to the late-January high (~$1.3865). Despite the strong retail sales and firm PMI, it has barely benefited. Cable has held below $1.3485. It must resurface above $1.3510 to improve the technical tone. The next area of support is seen around $1.3400 and then $1.3340-50. Sterling’s roughly 1.3% loss this week, if sustained today, would be the largest since January 2025.

• The US dollar traded at its best level against the Canadian dollar in nearly two weeks shortly after Canada reported a smaller-than-expected December merchandise trade deficit and the US reported a somewhat larger deficit. Still, excluding the pandemic distortion, Canada’s trade shortfall (~C$313 bln) was a record. Overall exports fell by 0.2%, led by a 5.8% drop in exports to the US. The greenback reached CAD1.3715 and remained above about CAD1.3680 for the remainder of the session. That range is largely holding today, but the Canadian dollar still looks vulnerable.

• A hawkish-leaning central bank and labor market data helped the Australian dollar minimize its losses against the firmer US dollar. With about a 0.35% decline this week, it is the best-performing G10 currency against the dollar. It kissed the 20-day moving average ($0.7025) yesterday for the first time in a month. It extended the losses to $0.7015 today before it recovered to a little above $0.7065. The momentum indicators are turning lower, warning of continued near-term consolidation. A break of the $0.6990-0.7000 level would suggest an outright correction is at hand.

EM

• After setting a new low against the Mexican peso since mid-2024 on Wednesday near MXN17.0865, the greenback has recovered. It poked above MXN17.30 yesterday for the first time in almost two weeks and settled above the 20-day moving average (~MXN17.26). It is consolidating quietly ahead of the North American session. Most of the Latam currencies fell this week, with the Argentine peso a notable exception. The month’s high was recorded on February 2 (~MXN17.5725).

• The dollar forged a base against the offshore yuan ahead of CNH6.88 in the first few days of the week. It approached Monday’s high yesterday as it traded near CNH6.9055. The greenback is in an exceptionally narrow range today, ~CNH6.8975-6.9045. It was trading around CNH6.90 when the mainland markets shut for the extended holiday.

• After yesterday’s holiday, the Indian rupee was sold to a new low for the month, and the central bank reportedly intervened in both the onshore and offshore markets. The dollar opened well above the recent highs and rose to INR90.9925, its best level since February 2. The rise in oil prices weighed on the local bond market as well as the currency.

Other Markets

• Equities are mixed today. In the Asia-Pacific region, while Tokyo (NKY:IND) lost over 1%, the surge in South Korea’s Kospi (KOSPI) continued. It rose 2.3% today to bring this week’s advance to 9.5%. Hong Kong (HSI) and mainland shares that trade there fell by 1.10-1.20%. Europe’s STOXX 600 (STOXX) is firm, recovering about half of yesterday’s 0.55% loss. US index futures (SPX) are narrowly mixed.

• Benchmark 10-year yields are mostly lower. The soft national CPI saw JGBs extend their rally. The yield has fallen for four consecutive weeks. It is off 10 bp this week, the most since April 2025. European yields are mostly lower, led by the two-basis point decline in UK Gilts. The 10-year US Treasury yield (US10Y) is flat, near 4.07%. It is up about two basis points ahead of US data.

• Gold rose to a new high for the week near $5042.50, which is slightly above last week’s settlement. It is near $5027 in late European morning turnover. The same is true of silver. It reached a new high for the week, near $81.20, and is around $80.50.

• April WTI edged higher initially, extending yesterday’s gain, and poked briefly above $67, where sellers emerged and pushed it back to around $66. It settled at $62.75 last week. Many are trying to game out the timing of attack scenarios, trying to take into account the lunar phase for ambient light, the Ramadan holiday (extends to March 19/20), and the like.

Data

• There are four highlights for the US today. The first estimate for Q4 GDP is due, and the median forecast in Bloomberg’s survey has crept up to 3% at an annualized rate. The price deflators are expected to have softened. The December personal income and consumption data are due with the deflators, which are expected to have held steady or ticked up slightly. The preliminary February PMI is due, and the composite looks little changed around 53.0. The last highlight, but it could be the most market-impactful, is the Supreme Court’s decision day. It does not pre-announce which cases it will rule on, but there is a chance that the verdict about the president’s use of emergency powers to levy widespread tariffs will be handed down.

• Canada reports December retail sales. The headline was likely dragged down by weaker vehicle sales.

• Mexico also reports December retail sales. After a 1.0% jump in November, sales likely slipped a little at the end of the year.

• The preliminary February eurozone PMI improved. The manufacturing PMI rose from 49.5 to 50.8, its highest reading since June 2022. New orders rose to 50.9 from 49.2, which is the highest since April 2022. However, new export orders remained below the 50 boom/bust level for the eighth consecutive month. Germany’s manufacturing PMI rose above 50 for the first time in three and a half years, while the French manufacturing PMI slipped back below 50 (49.9) after holding above 50 for the previous two months. The aggregate service PMI edged up for the first time in three months (51.9 from 51.6). Last year’s peak was in November at 53.6. The composite PMI rose to 51.9 from 51.3 in January.

• The busy week for UK data ended today with an unexpected jump in retail sales and mixed preliminary February PMI. January retail sales jumped 1.8% (median forecast in Bloomberg’s survey was for a 0.2% increase) after December’s 0.4% increase. Excluding gasoline, retail sales surged 2.0% following December’s 0.3% rise. The flash composite PMI ticked up 53.9 from 53.7 in January, which was the strongest since April 2024. The manufacturing PMI firmed to 52.0 from 51.8, while services growth eased slightly (53.9 vs. 54.0). When everything has been said and done, the swaps market anticipates a year-end rate of about 3.23%, which implies two cuts this year.

• Australia’s preliminary February PMI softened. The composite eased to 52.0 from 55.7 in January. It was 51.0 at the end of last year and 50.6 in February 2025. The manufacturing PMI stands at 51.5 (52.3 in January), while the service PMI is at 52.2 (56.3 in January).

• The Tokyo CPI that was reported a few weeks ago signaled the decline in Japan’s January CPI. The headline rate fell to 1.5% from 2.1%, and the core rate, which excludes fresh food, fell to the 2.0% target from 2.4% in December. Excluding fresh food and energy, consumer inflation remained sticky at 2.6% (2.9% in December). Separately, the preliminary February composite PMI rose to 53.8 from 53.1 in January. The manufacturing PMI is at 52.8 (51.5 in January), and the services PMI stands at 53.8 (53.7 in January).

• India’s February PMIs continued to show a strong start to the year. The composite PMI is 59.3 (58.4 in January), which is higher than the manufacturing PMI (57.5) and the services PMI (58.4). The Reserve Bank of India projects the economy will expand by 7.3% in the current fiscal year, up from 6.5% in FY25.

data.title: John Hancock 2055 Lifetime Blend Portfolio Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market review and outlook

Global equities registered solid gains in the fourth quarter, helping the major, broad-based indexes record their third consecutive year of double-digit returns. Performance was uneven over the first half of the quarter due to concerns that AI-related stocks were in a bubble, but the market staged an impressive rebound and went on to achieve new all-time highs by year-end. A continued decline in inflation enabled the U.S. Federal Reserve to enact two quarter-point interest rate cuts, boosting sentiment. In addition, corporate earnings were robust and world economic growth remained positive. Emerging- and developed-market international equities outperformed the United States, continuing a trend that was in place for the full year. Within the U.S. market, the value style outpaced growth as investors rotated toward opportunities outside of AI-related stocks.

Global bonds logged only slightly positive total returns amid a growing consensus that most central banks were largely finished easing policy. Credit-oriented market segments continued to outperform, primarily as a result of their yield advantage.

Contributors and detractors

The fund's overweight in equities versus bonds contributed to relative performance. An overweight in emerging-market stocks, the best-performing segment of the global equity markets in the quarter, was a further plus. On the other hand, the allocation to defensive equities was a modest detractor at a time in which more speculative stocks were in favor. An overweight in U.S. mid caps also cost the fund some relative performance. Positioning in fixed income had a neutral effect on results. An allocation to emerging-market bonds performed well, as the asset class strongly outperformed its developed-market peers in an environment of falling interest rates and rising commodity prices. Conversely, a position in U.S. Treasury STRIPS detracted amid a broader increase in long-term bond yields.

No single area stood out as a key driver of underperformance for the fund's active managers; instead, the shortfall was primarily a result of very small differences for a wide range of individual holdings.

Portfolio changes

We retained diversified exposure across the full spectrum of the world financial markets, while also making some modest changes on the margin to capitalize on categories where we saw attractive valuations and higher long-term return potential. We remain confident in the fund's positioning as we move into 2026, based on our belief that diversification is likely to become increasingly important. For most of the past year, investors would have been rewarded by simply holding the narrow group of mega-cap U.S. technology-related stocks that continued to dominate performance. However, we began to see this backdrop evolve somewhat over the final three months of the year with a notable broadening of leadership. While it's impossible to say if this is a sign of a larger shift, we believe investors are better served by maintaining broad-based diversification rather than relying on another year of positive relative performance for a handful of companies. We think the fund is well positioned for these circumstances through its ability to seek opportunities across a wide range of asset classes.

data.title: Star Holdings: A Liquidating Security Approaching Capital Returns Phase
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the McIntyre Partnerships Q4 2025 Partners Letter.

Star Holdings (STHO) is a liquidating security in which the fund has been invested since 2023. Its primary assets are 13.3MM SAFE shares and liquidating real estate in Asbury Park and Richmond. It is a very low-liquidity security, where the stock's price and fundamental value can move in opposite directions purely due to liquidity. After several years with minimal updates from the company, I believe that has happened here. However, STHO has made significant progress toward liquidation over the past three years, and I believe the company will announce significant capital returns in 2026.

When it was spun off from its former parent, iStar, now called Safehold (SAFE), STHO was envisioned as a three- to five-year liquidation, with certain hard-to-sell assets placed in the spinco to simplify the larger company's investment story. We are now entering year four of this plan. These assets were not spun off because they were troubled, as is typical in "bad bank" spins, but rather because they were real estate developments that do not match SAFE's mandate to invest in ground leases. Since the spin, STHO has made significant progress, realizing sales of ~$170MM of real estate versus their book value of ~$120MM. STHO now holds only $113MM of land for development, down from $224MM at the time of spin. Further, public filings in Asbury Park indicate that several land parcels have been optioned by large builders such as Hovnanian and Toll Brothers, yet they remain on STHO's balance sheet. I anticipate most of these lots will move towards construction in 2026. STHO has also substantially reduced the management fee owed to SAFE, from ~$55MM at the time of spin to ~$10MM today. To facilitate these asset sales, STHO engaged in numerous seller financings. While initially a drain on cash, STHO has successfully recuperated all of these when due, and I anticipate several key repayments in 2026, including the elimination of the Surfhouse JV, which has made STHO's financials difficult to understand. These actions have substantially strengthened STHO's balance sheet. At year-end 2025, I calculate STHO's cash and readily marketable bond investments at ~$94MM versus their credit facility at $115MM.

Which brings us to STHO's largest and most important asset – its 13.3MM SAFE shares. At the time of the spin, STHO had significant asset value but limited cash. To safely capitalize the spinco, management spun it off with shares of the parent company, SAFE, which could be sold if needed. As I calculate STHO at ~$21MM of net debt, versus my expectations for >$50MM of cash flow from loan repayments and asset sales in 2026, the logic for maintaining their significant SAFE stake is weakening. I anticipate STHO announcing either a spin-off or sale of their SAFE shares in 2026, along with a repayment or restructuring of their existing credit facility. As STHO's SAFE shares have a current value of ~$120MM, net of a $91MM margin loan, a distribution to shareholders would be ~$10 per share, versus STHO's current $8 price.

While some investors may question whether management is committed to returning capital, hence the discount in STHO's shares, I have had positive discussions with management. They are also significant shareholders. CEO Jay Sugarman owns ~4% of shares outstanding. More importantly, STHO is already returning capital to shareholders via share buybacks. In 2025, STHO repurchased ~9% of shares outstanding.

If STHO continues to make progress and returns capital to shareholders, I believe our ultimate realization for STHO should be $20-$30/sh. versus a current price of $8.

data.title: QQQM: Capturing AI Growth Doesn't Have To Be Complicated
data.description:
Overview

If you're a follower of mine, you'll know that I almost exclusively publish analyses related to dividend stocks and high-yield funds. I previously published an article titled 'A Warning To Young Dividend Investors', which highlighted some of the mistakes I made along my investing journey from a decade ago. As a dividend investor, you may dismiss many high quality companies that don't offer the best dividend value. However, I believe that ignoring the potential growth of the technology sector in this era of AI would be a mistake. Therefore, I wanted to cover the Invesco NASDAQ 100 ETF (QQQM), which I believe to be one of the most straightforward ways to participate in the expansion of AI.

Looking at the performance of the ETF, we can see that QQQM has delivered a total return of more than 12.3% over the last twelve months. The fund offers a small dividend yield of 0.5% while providing exposure to many of the highest quality companies in the world. However, QQQM is the younger sibling to the more popular Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ). Despite this, I believe that QQQM is the better holding for long-term investors that want to implement a buy-and-hold strategy.

Sentiment in the technology sector can shift quickly so my approach has always been to exclusively remain exposed to the large-cap players in the space. These tend to be businesses with diversified revenue streams that can produce consistent cash flows to help navigate any headwinds that AI faces. This reduces the speculative nature of the sector and makes it a lot less stressful for investors. Although I am bullish on technology, I believe the path forward will be filled with uncertainty and periods of elevated volatility, like we are currently experiencing. However, QQQM is a great way to navigate potential risks since it provides a healthy level of diversity across the Nasdaq-100 index.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fund overview, QQQM has total assets under management of $71.1B that are spread across the highest quality businesses in the index. The fund has a listed expense ratio of 0.15%, which makes it slightly more cost-efficient than QQQ's expense ratio of 0.20%. There isn't much of a difference between QQQM and QQQ, except for the size of the funds and their expense ratio. QQQ may present better liquidity and the ability to trade options against it, but in terms of strategy and holdings the funds look quite similar.

Under normal market conditions, the fund will invest at least 90% of its assets into stocks within the Nasdaq-100 Index (NDX). QQQM rebalances its holdings quarterly, while initiating reconstitution of its holdings annually. Since the fund essentially mirrors the index, the allocation by sector looks similar. For instance, QQQM is most heavily allocated to the technology sector at 51.62% of assets. I actually like the fact that QQQM isn't entirely allocated to technology stocks because this helps the fund participate in the sector's growth but with the potential for less volatility. This is followed by an allocation to communication and consumer cyclical companies, which accounts for 15.89% and 12.82% of assets respectively.

When looking at the top holdings of QQQM, we can see that the ETF is a bit top heavy. Although QQQM has exposure to 102 different positions, the top ten holdings make up ~49.5% of total assets. For instance, Nvidia (NVDA) is the largest holding with a weight of 9.04%. This is followed by exposure to many other high quality companies, including Apple (AAPL), Microsoft (MSFT), Amazon (AMZN), Tesla (TSLA), and Meta Platforms (META), just to name a few. As we can see, QQQM essentially provides exposure to the Mag 7, which means that investors can directly participate in the growth of these companies.

An ETF like QQQM is always an efficient long-term holding because an investor can instantly get diversity across the strongest businesses in the world. By investing in a basket of these companies, we can eliminate some of the speculation, risks, and guess work involved with selecting individual positions. This diversity also means that we get exposure to some of the strong momentum companies, such as Palantir (PLTR), which trade at massive premium valuations, but continue to rise over time due to their positive market sentiment.

Outlook

It's hard to talk about the outlook of technology and AI without coming across as hyperbolic. However, I truly believe that this is one of the most interesting opportunities that the market has provided in the last decade. From a consumer's perspective, I already enjoy the many forms of advances that were influenced by AI within my entertainment avenues like video games or movies, while also seeing how LLMs (Large Language Models) like Gemini or ChatGPT can be used to enhance productivity and creativity within various forms of media.

From an investor's perspective, I believe that the growth of companies may not necessarily come from the products or services that are the result of AI. While this already does exist, such as Alphabet's (GOOG) Gemini subscription revenue, I believe that the real potential is how AI will improve operating efficiency for companies. I believe that we will see companies efficiently decouple their headcount from the revenue growth and costs will be cut to levels that were once unimaginable. While this may be some years away, the idea isn't too far-fetched as we already see plenty of examples of this.

I personally believe that Amazon will be one of the clearest examples of this over time. The business already has great momentum with the 24% growth of its AWS segment, but I think their recent restructuring will serve as the blueprint that many other companies will follow. For instance, Amazon has initiated several rounds of layoffs to trim capital as their capex spending increases. The business may be shifting to focus on capital efficiency, which has the potential to massively improve their operating margins. CEO, Andy Jassy, has already stated that generative AI and agents will replace the workforce. If Amazon is successful in their execution, we may see other large caps follow their lead.

We will need fewer people doing some of the jobs that are being done today, and more people doing other types of jobs. -- It’s hard to know exactly where this nets out over time, but in the next few years, we expect that this will reduce our total corporate workforce as we get efficiency gains from using AI extensively across the company.

Furthermore, the projected growth of the size of AI sector looks attractive. At the close of 2025, the size of the AI market was valued around $391B. Grand View Research currently estimates that the size of the AI market can increase as high as ~$3.5T by 2033. This represents a strong CAGR (compound annual growth rate) of 30.6% through 2033. Even if this estimate is half-wrong, this would still represent a massive double-digit growth potential over the next several years. Since QQQM has direct exposure to the businesses leading the AI charge forward, this is one of the clearest ways for investors to participate.

Risks

As we've seen over the last few weeks, the market isn't a big fan of the massive capex spending levels that businesses are initiating. In order to fulfill the growing demand, many of the large-cap leaders are allocating record-breaking levels of cash towards the build out of their AI infrastructure. Just to help visualize how large spending levels have become, here are some estimated capex spending amounts from this most recent earnings season:

U.S. Hyperscale Capex Estimates (2026 Fiscal Guidance)

  • Amazon: ~$200B
  • Alphabet: ~$175B - ~$185B
  • Microsoft ~$140B
  • Meta Platforms: ~$115B - ~$135B

These massive capex spending levels have investors concerned on whether or not this will actually lead to higher earnings growth in the near-term. As a result, there has been a surge in short bets within the market. There's always the risk that these different AI initiatives take longer than anticipated or cost more than originally forecast.

Furthermore, data compiled by Evercore ISI Research indicates the plummeting free cash flow levels this capex spending has caused. This data says that the free cash flow levels have fallen to the 'yellow flag' areas that were present during the lows of the 2022 cycle. These analysts are essentially saying that they believe businesses need to substantially improve cash on their balance sheets, rather than committing more capital towards capex spending.

However, I believe these risks are a bit overstated since the Mag 7 companies that I just listed are some of the most profitable businesses in the world. I actually think that the front-loading of capital to build out their infrastructure can be a benefit. Companies tend not to aggressively increase capex unless they see a clear rise in demand or an end-goal. Additionally, many of these same businesses just reported healthy earnings, which I believe should offset concerns. For instance, META reported that its revenue increased 24%, beating expectations.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I believe that QQQM remains a very straightforward way for investors to obtain exposure to some of the leaders within the technology sector. QQQM is cost-efficient with a low expense ratio and the diversity across businesses means that investors are able to participate in the expansion of the market with less risk than holding individual positions. The market seems to be worried about the large capex spending levels but I believe these risks are overdone. Many of the large-cap leaders in the sector continue to report strong earnings growth and the build out of AI infrastructure will better align these businesses to capture the rising demand of the sector.

data.title: Sotera Health: A Predictable And Growing Business
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the McIntyre Partnerships Q4 2025 Partners Letter.

Sotera Health's (SHC) stock was volatile in 2025 but ultimately finished strong. Despite the share price volatility, SHC remains a predictable, growing, and recession-proof business. These are exactly the attributes we look for in a core holding. Here are the company's financials over the last few years:

Despite the noise, the core business remains a stable franchise that has consistently grown sales and operating profits with zero down years in the last 20 years.

Further, 2025 marked an inflection year, with the core Sterigenics segment posting 10% revenue growth, up from ~4-7% in the previous two years. This inflection was driven by Sterigenics finally lapping the inventory destocking that hospitals and distributors saw following COVID-19 hoarding, which lifted volumes from flat to ~5% growth. With destocking in the rearview mirror, SHC is well positioned to return to its historical 10%+ EBITDA growth rate, which is above Street estimates and, I believe, represents a significant catalyst.

In addition, SHC should benefit from the tax law changes in the OBIB Act. SHC has been paying a ~34% tax rate because the 2017 tax law lowered the interest deductibility cap from 30% of EBITDA to 30% of EBIT in 2022. The new tax law restores deductibility to EBITDA, which, combined with SHC's growth and debt paydown, will reduce SHC's tax rate to the mid-twenties. However, despite the change in the law, the Street still models SHC's tax rate around 32%. When SHC reports, I anticipate guidance confirming the lowered tax rate, which should boost EPS estimates by ~10%.

SHC also saw solid developments on the legal front. They continued to make progress and settled numerous cases in line with previous settlements. Further, SHC saw a significant number of cases dismissed in Georgia for failure to prove causation, which should reduce the future value of settlements in the state. While I anticipate SHC will continue to face litigation and pay settlements, I believe the largest settlements are behind them, and further settlements, like the $31MM in April 2025, are minor bumps rather than material headwinds.

Despite improved volumes, changes in tax law, and positive legal developments, SHC remains at a material discount to STE, the other side of their sterilization duopoly. For comparison, STE trades at ~25x 2026 EPS and 21x 2027 EPS, versus SHC at 17x and 14x my estimates, respectively. Given the inflecting growth and discount to peers, we retain a large position. I believe our investment is positioned to benefit from SHC's ~10% EBITDA growth per year, continuing capital returns, and potential multiple expansion. I value SHC at 25x my 2027 EPS estimate of $1.20, implying a $30 valuation versus its current $17 price.

data.title: Fastly: Improved Demand, But The Company Remains Flawed
data.description:
Fastly's (FSLY) share price surged after the company reported Q4 2025 results that showed a surprisingly large growth acceleration. While Network Services revenue growth was solid, supported by emerging AI tailwinds, Security and edge compute are becoming increasingly important contributors.

I previously suggested that there were positive shifts occurring within Fastly's business. Despite a low valuation and potential near-term tailwinds, I was cautious about the stock, though, as Fastly is a flawed company due to its narrow platform vision and focus on relatively commoditized markets.

While pricing pressure has temporarily eased, the CDN market structure remains unchanged. In addition, Fastly continues to face customer concentration risk and a lack of customer growth. There is also a risk that Security growth will moderate as the boost provided by the adoption of newer products fades. As a result, I am fairly neutral on Fastly's prospects, despite the likelihood of strong growth and improving margins over the next 12 months.

Market Conditions

Internet traffic increased by an estimated 16% YoY in 2025, with growth supported by AI and agentic traffic. In addition, the shift of sports and other live events to streaming is a tailwind as it contributes to traffic spikes.

While CDN prices have historically declined 5% annually on average, Fastly has suggested that in recent years prices have declined by a double-digit percentage annually. A stronger demand environment and market consolidation have now created more supportive pricing conditions, though. This includes Edgio (formerly Limelight) filing for bankruptcy, Akamai (AKAM) acquiring Linode and Lumen, and StackPath selling their CDN assets. Price erosion in the fourth quarter was reportedly in the mid-single digits.

Investor focus is likely to be more on Fastly's security and edge compute businesses going forward, though, because of the opportunities that AI is creating (bot management, API security, edge inferencing). Fastly isn't as well positioned to benefit from AI and agent traffic as Cloudflare (NET), though, due to the company's fairly narrow platform vision. I believe that this is critical because of the margin benefits that diverse traffic provides.

Fastly Business Updates

Fastly introduced a bot mitigation solution and made its adaptive DDoS protection solution generally available in 2024, giving the company a complete security portfolio (bot mitigation, WAF, DDoS prevention). This now appears to be paying off, as the company's Security revenue growth has accelerated significantly over the past 12 months. Broader platform adoption should also help to strengthen the company's competitive positioning and reduce Network Services pricing pressure. There is a risk that this is just a one-off growth boost as adoption rises among existing customers, though. Even if this does prove to be the case, there should still be a considerable growth runway, as only a single-digit percentage of Fastly's customers had adopted its DDoS or bot mitigation solutions in early 2025.

Fastly's Other segment is also a growth driver at the moment, which largely relates to its edge compute solution. Compute enables Fastly to provide dynamic real-time experiences, particularly now that the company offers storage. Fastly believes that edge compute will support the expansion of its business in strategic verticals, like travel and leisure, technology, financial services, and retail.

Much of Fastly's recent focus has also been on a go-to-market transformation, designed to accelerate the acquisition of new logos. For example, Fastly introduced a self-service solution and is using mix-and-match packages with simplified pricing to drive adoption. Weak growth of the customer base should be viewed as particularly disappointing in light of this. The company also has a high-touch customer success motion for large media accounts, which is designed to drive traffic share growth among its largest customers. Strong Network Services and RPO growth indicate that Fastly is having more success with this initiative.

Financial Analysis

Fastly generated $172.6 million in revenue in the fourth quarter, an increase of 23% YoY. Network Services growth is reportedly being driven by increased traffic and share gains within customers, as indicated by Fastly's strength relative to Akamai.

While growth was robust across Fastly's segments, Security is a growth driver at the moment, supported by customers adopting newer products. Edge compute growth also remains strong, although this is still a fairly small part of Fastly's business.

It is worth noting that Fastly lapped the loss of a large media company in the second quarter, meaning easier comparables have contributed to the improvement in growth.

While churn remains very low, Fastly still hasn't been able to meaningfully expand its customer base. The company had 3,092 total customers and 628 enterprise customers in the fourth quarter. This means that growth is coming from expansion within existing customers, as demonstrated by the company's 110% LTM NRR. On a positive note, Fastly has realized very large RPO increases in recent quarters, indicating that larger customers are committing to the platform.

Fastly is guiding to $168-174 million in revenue in the first quarter, representing an 18% YoY increase at the midpoint. For the full year, the company expects $700-720 million in revenue, representing 14% growth at the midpoint. Guidance is fairly soft, but Fastly isn't extrapolating recent trends due to macro uncertainty. This likely means that growth will handily beat expectations in the near term.

Infrastructure CapEx will rise to 10–12% of revenue in 2026, which supports the notion of strong growth in 2026. Fastly's hiring remains depressed, though, indicating that the company is still cautious about investing ahead of expected growth.

In addition to stronger growth, Fastly has been able to achieve impressive margin gains over the past few years. This is supported by Security and edge compute growth, both of which carry higher gross margins. Gross margin improvements have also been supported by a more favorable pricing environment for Network Services. While the CDN market has consolidated, a less robust demand environment could see pricing pressure return.

Fastly is targeting 75-80% of incremental revenue falling through to gross profit and 35-40% falling through to operating profit going forward, which should see the company transition to profitability over the next few years.

Conclusion

Fastly's share price has shot higher in recent weeks, supported by improving margins and a significant growth reacceleration. Growth is being driven by an improved demand environment and success in security products (WAF, Bot Management, etc.). Edge compute growth also remains robust, and this part of the business should become more material over the next few years.

While this may seem like a positive setup, Fastly continues to have a concentrated customer base, which it is struggling to expand. The company is also still heavily dependent on relatively commoditized Network Services. In addition, there is a risk that Security growth proves temporary as the boost provided by newer products fades. As a result, I am fairly neutral on Fastly's prospects now that its revenue multiple is back above 4x.
